title: A book for a story
summary: In a second-hand bookshop in Cape Town, the owner sometimes accepts a story instead of money.

body.speaker: Daniel
body.text: Excuse me. How much is this poetry book?

body.speaker: Esther
body.text: Forty rand. But you do not look like you have forty rand on you. Am I wrong?

body.speaker: Daniel
body.text: You are not wrong. I have eighteen.

body.speaker: Esther
body.text: I do not lower prices. But I sometimes take stories. If you have a true story about why you want this book, I will trade.

body.speaker: Esther
body.text: True. Not long. Three minutes. I will know if it is not true.

body.speaker: Daniel
body.text: My grandmother had this book. She died last year. I do not have her copy. My uncle threw away her things. I came here every Saturday for three months looking for the same book. I found this one today.

body.speaker: Daniel
body.text: I have eighteen rand. Let me give you eighteen.

body.speaker: Esther
body.text: No. Today I take only the story. Next time you can pay.

body.speaker: Esther
body.text: Because somebody did this for me when I was twenty-three. I bought my first novel for a story. That novel is on my shelf, behind you. I keep one promise to her. I pass it on when somebody comes who needs the same thing.

body.speaker: Esther
body.text: A woman who ran a small shop in Johannesburg. She is dead now. The shop is gone. But the rule is still alive. You are part of it now. One day, if you are running anything — a shop, a class, a meeting — you do the same. Once. You will know when.

body.speaker: Esther
body.text: Good. Now take the book and go before I change my mind.

annotations.meaningVi: đồng rand (đơn vị tiền tệ của Nam Phi)
vứt bỏ — past simple của 'throw away'
truyền tiếp / chuyển giao tiếp — cụm động từ
đang điều hành bất cứ gì (run = điều hành)
hiệu sách cũ / sách đã qua sử dụng
ai đó đã làm điều này cho tôi

grammarNotes.title: Conditional 'if' clauses (zero / first conditional)
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu điều kiện loại 1 (first conditional) diễn tả khả năng có thật trong tương lai: If + present simple, will + V. Trong bài: 'If you have a true story about why you want this book, I will trade.' (Nếu cậu có một câu chuyện thật, tôi sẽ đổi). 'One day, if you are running anything — a shop, a class, a meeting — you do the same.' (zero conditional: dùng cho sự thật / quy luật chung; cả hai vế đều ở present). Phân biệt: present + will (khả năng tương lai) vs. present + present (quy luật).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: First conditional describes a realistic future possibility: If + present simple, will + base verb. From the dialogue: 'If you have a true story about why you want this book, I will trade.' Also a zero-conditional pattern: 'One day, if you are running anything..., you do the same.' Zero conditional (present + present) marks general rules; first conditional (present + will) marks specific future possibilities.

translationVi:
Daniel: Xin lỗi. Quyển thơ này bao nhiêu?
Esther: Bốn mươi rand. Nhưng nhìn cậu không có đủ bốn mươi rand trong người. Tôi nói sai không?
Daniel: Cô không sai. Cháu có mười tám.
Esther: Tôi không hạ giá. Nhưng đôi khi tôi nhận chuyện kể. Nếu cậu có một câu chuyện thật về lý do muốn quyển sách này, tôi sẽ đổi.
Daniel: Một câu chuyện thật ạ?
Esther: Thật. Không dài. Ba phút. Tôi sẽ biết nếu nó không thật.
Daniel: Bà cháu có quyển này. Bà mất năm ngoái. Cháu không giữ được quyển của bà. Cậu cháu vứt đồ của bà đi rồi. Cháu đến đây mỗi thứ Bảy ba tháng nay để tìm cùng một cuốn. Hôm nay cháu tìm được.
Esther: Lấy đi.
Daniel: Cháu có mười tám rand. Cháu đưa cô mười tám.
Esther: Không. Hôm nay tôi chỉ nhận câu chuyện. Lần sau cậu trả cũng được.
Daniel: Sao vậy ạ?
Esther: Vì có người đã làm điều này cho tôi khi tôi hai mươi ba tuổi. Tôi mua cuốn tiểu thuyết đầu tiên bằng một câu chuyện. Cuốn đó ở trên kệ phía sau cậu. Tôi giữ một lời hứa với bà ấy. Tôi truyền lại khi có ai đến mà cần điều tương tự.
Daniel: Bà ấy là ai vậy cô?
Esther: Một người phụ nữ điều hành tiệm nhỏ ở Johannesburg. Bà mất rồi. Tiệm đóng rồi. Nhưng quy ước vẫn còn. Bây giờ cậu là một phần của nó. Một ngày nào đó, nếu cậu điều hành bất cứ thứ gì — một tiệm, một lớp, một cuộc họp — cậu hãy làm điều tương tự. Một lần thôi. Cậu sẽ biết lúc nào.
Daniel: Cháu sẽ làm.
Esther: Tốt. Giờ cầm sách và đi đi, trước khi tôi đổi ý.

questions.prompt: How much is the poetry book?
What is Esther's offer instead of lowering the price?
options: She accepts a true three-minute story
She accepts work in the shop

questions.prompt: How will Esther judge the story?
options: She will know if it is not true
questions.explanation: 'I will know if it is not true.'

questions.prompt: What is Daniel's story about the book?
options: His grandmother had this book; she died; his uncle threw away her things; he has searched for three months
He wants to give it as a wedding gift

questions.prompt: Does Esther take the eighteen rand he offers?
options: No — she takes only the story today
Yes — she takes all of it

questions.prompt: When did somebody do this kindness for Esther?
Where did that woman run her shop?
What does Esther call this practice?
options: A rule that is still alive even though the shop is gone

questions.prompt: What does she ask Daniel to do one day?
options: When he is running anything (shop, class, meeting), do the same — once
'Take the book and go before I change my mind.'
She gives him his money back.
A small kindness can survive longer than the people and places that started it, if each receiver passes it on once.

cloze.template:
Daniel: Excuse me. How much is this poetry book?
Esther: Forty rand. But you do not look like you have forty rand on you. Am I {{b1}}?
Daniel: You are not wrong. I have eighteen.
Esther: I do not lower prices. But I sometimes take stories. If you have a true {{b2}} about why you want this book, I will trade.
Daniel: A true story?
Esther: True. Not long. Three minutes. I will know if it is not {{b3}}.
Daniel: My grandmother had this book. She died last year. I do not have her copy. My uncle {{b4}} away her things. I came here every Saturday for three months looking for the same book. I found this one today.
Esther: Take it.
Daniel: I have eighteen rand. Let me give you eighteen.
Esther: No. Today I take only the story. Next time you can {{b5}}.
Daniel: Why?
Esther: Because somebody did this for me when I was twenty-three. I bought my first novel for a story. That novel is on my {{b6}}, behind you. I keep one {{b7}} to her. I pass it on when somebody comes who needs the same thing.
Daniel: Who was she?
Esther: A woman who ran a small shop in Johannesburg. She is dead now. The shop is gone. But the rule is still {{b8}}. You are part of it now. One day, if you are running anything — a shop, a class, a meeting — you do the {{b9}}. Once. You will know when.
Daniel: I will.
Esther: Good. Now take the book and go before I change my {{b10}}.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Esther says, 'The shop is gone. But the rule is still alive.' Is there a small kindness someone once did for you that you have never repaid in their direction — but could pass on once, sideways, to a stranger? Who might that stranger be, and what is your version of 'the book'?
